title: Loss of a Loved One
description: Processing grief and recovery after loss.
welcomeMessage: Find support in the grieving process. Learn to process loss and move forward while preserving memory of your loved one.

cards.introduction: After a loss, your mind may try to “hold it together”: work, solve problems, be the strong one. Many men know this mode well. Inside, grief can come in waves—numbness, heaviness, anger, a lump in the throat, unreality. That’s a normal nervous‑system response, not you being broken. The first step isn’t “get over it”. It’s naming what’s happening in you. In this card we’ll give grief a shape so it becomes a bit more bearable.
cards.shortDescription: Grief comes in waves and can feel like numbness or heaviness. We’ll name emotions and thoughts to reduce inner tension.
questions: In the last 7 days, when did a memory hit the hardest? Describe 1–2 moments. Rate intensity 1–10 and add what you felt in your body.
What thoughts came next? Write 2–3 lines word for word. What do they make you do—shut down, overwork, avoid people?
cards.recommendation:
Acknowledging emotion reduces the inner fight. When you stop pushing it away, it becomes more “passable” and less explosive.

Do this (10 minutes, 3 times this week):
1) Set a timer for 10 minutes.
2) Name: “Right now I feel…” and “In my body it feels like…”
3) Let it be there; return to breath when thoughts pull you away.
4) End with one line: “This wave was X/10. I got through it.”

If 10 minutes is too much, start with 3. Dosing matters.
cards.mechanism: Acceptance reduces avoidance and physiological tension.

cards.introduction: Guilt after loss is common: “I should have…”, “I didn’t have time…”. For men, responsibility can make it worse—like you were supposed to prevent it. Often guilt is your mind trying to regain control over something uncontrollable. It feels like “if it’s my fault, I could have fixed it.” The price is self‑punishment. In this card we’ll shift perspective: look at you through a friend’s eyes and bring fairness back.
cards.shortDescription: Guilt often hides helplessness and a need for control. We’ll shift perspective to reduce self-punishment.
questions: Do you have thoughts like 'I could have...', 'I didn't have time...'?
What would you say to a friend in this situation?
cards.recommendation:
Perspective shift softens self-attack. You don’t remove pain—you stop adding punishment.

Do this (10 minutes):
1) Write a letter to yourself from a good friend.
2) Include: the loss is real; you couldn’t control everything; one supportive line.
3) End with: “You can live on and still remember.”

If writing is hard, start with 5 sentences.
cards.mechanism: Perspective shift reduces destructive guilt and self-criticism.

cards.introduction: Avoiding memories can feel like survival: it hurts too much. Many men cope by “doing things” and not talking about it. The downside is that avoidance often makes waves stronger later. Memory can hold both pain and warmth. In this card you’ll choose one safe “anchor” (a photo, object, song) and practise being with it in a controlled, short way—without flooding yourself.
cards.shortDescription: Avoidance can intensify grief waves. We’ll use one safe memory “anchor” to reconnect without overload.
questions: What memories simultaneously cause pain and gratitude?
What of these memories would you like to preserve in yourself?
cards.recommendation:
Conscious contact with memory helps integration: pain remains, but meaning and warmth can sit next to it.

Do this:
1) Pick one anchor (photo/object/song).
2) Set 5 minutes; decide you’ll stop if it rises above 8/10.
3) Notice: what I feel, where in my body, one warm detail I remember.
4) Close: water/tea + 3 long exhales.

If it’s too heavy, shorten to 1–2 minutes.
cards.mechanism: Gradual memory exposure reduces avoidance and stabilises emotional response.

cards.introduction: Sometimes the pain holds because something was left unsaid: gratitude, apology, a question, a goodbye. Men often try to “close the topic” by staying silent, but unfinished words keep tension inside. Symbolic completion can help. In this card you’ll write a letter—not for drama, but to unload what you’ve been carrying and give your mind a sense of closure.
cards.shortDescription: Unsaid words can keep tension alive. We’ll use a letter as a symbolic closure to reduce inner load.
questions: What would you like to say to this person — but didn't have time?
What words would you like to hear from them now?
cards.recommendation:
Writing helps your mind complete an unfinished loop.

Do this:
1) Set 15 minutes.
2) Write by hand if possible.
3) Use structure: “I want to say… / I’m grateful for… / I’m sorry for… / I’m taking with me…”
4) Close the ritual: keep it in an envelope/box or destroy it—your choice.

If it overwhelms you, shorten the time and pause.
cards.mechanism: Symbolic completion reduces tension from unfinished communication.

cards.introduction: Grief isn’t weakness; it means the bond mattered. Many men fear they’ll “fall apart” if they let themselves feel it, so they avoid—and grief shows up as insomnia, tension, irritability. A helpful tool is a safe, time‑limited window for feelings. You don’t drown; you dose it. In this card you’ll create a small container for grief so it doesn’t run your life from the background.
cards.shortDescription: Avoiding grief often pushes it into the body. We’ll create a safe, time-limited window for feelings to reduce tension.
questions: What are you afraid of when you start feeling loss pain?
What would change if you allowed yourself to grieve?
cards.recommendation:
Time‑limited grieving reduces avoidance and lowers background tension.

Do this (3 times this week):
1) Timer 10–15 minutes.
2) Choose an anchor (music, photo, quiet space).
3) Let feelings move in waves.
4) Close: tea/shower/water + a few long exhales.

Start with 5 minutes if needed.
cards.mechanism: Dosed emotional processing reduces avoidance and physiological tension.

cards.introduction: After a loss, life can feel meaningless: goals stop pulling, days turn grey. That doesn’t mean you’re broken; it means your system is recalibrating. Overworking rarely brings meaning back. What helps early is returning to “alive” signals—small actions that bring your body into the present. In this card you’ll choose one simple “alive action” for 3 days, without demanding any special mood.
cards.shortDescription: Meaning can disappear after loss. We’ll restore contact with “alive” signals through small actions in the present.
questions: What started seeming 'unimportant' to you after the loss?
What small actions bring you at least some sense of life?
cards.recommendation:
This isn’t about “having fun”. It’s about bringing your nervous system back into the world.

Do this (3 days):
1) One 10‑minute “alive action” per day.
2) No phone during it.
3) End with: one thing you noticed (sound/smell/sensation).

Examples: a short walk, a hot meal, a shower with attention to temperature.
cards.mechanism: Present-moment contact reduces emptiness and dissociation.

cards.introduction: A bond doesn’t end overnight—it changes form: from external contact to inner support, memory, values. Some men try to “close it” to hurt less, but that can cut off meaning. There’s a stronger path: keep the connection in a way that helps you live. In this card you’ll pick one quality you valued in the person and express it through a weekly action—so memory becomes guidance, not only pain.
cards.shortDescription: Connection can become inner support. We’ll choose one valued quality and express it through action to keep meaning alive.
questions: What qualities of the departed person did you especially value?
How can you embody these qualities in your life?
cards.recommendation:
Value-based actions help integrate grief: pain stays, but meaning grows.

Do this (4 weeks):
1) Pick one quality.
2) Choose one weekly action that reflects it.
3) Do it and note how you feel before/after (1–10).
cards.mechanism: Values-based actions integrate memory and support meaning after loss.

cards.introduction: Returning to life can feel like betrayal: “If I laugh, I forgot.” Many men carry this silently and punish themselves by avoiding anything good. But memory and life can coexist. You can remember and still live. In this card you’ll write the message you wish you could hear from them—and honour it through one meaningful action, so guilt softens and direction returns.
cards.shortDescription: Living on isn’t betrayal. We’ll turn a supportive message into one meaningful action to reduce guilt.
questions: If this person could see you now, what would they wish for you?
How can you honor this message through your behavior?
cards.recommendation:
Meaningful action turns love into practice.

Do this:
1) Write the message.
2) Choose one 10–30 minute action that honours it.
3) Do it and rate guilt/warmth 1–10.

If guilt shows up, remind yourself: you’re not erasing memory; you’re carrying it through living.
cards.mechanism: Meaning-based action reduces guilt and supports integration.

cards.introduction: Loss changes a person. You may become more fragile, or more hardened, or more awake to what matters. Many men want to “go back to who I was” to regain control. But part of healing is building a new identity that holds both memory and life. In this card you’ll name what changed and choose what you want to keep—and what you want to soften—so you become the author of your next chapter.
cards.shortDescription: Loss changes you—and that’s normal. We’ll name the changes and choose what to keep and what to soften.
questions: What changed in you as a person after this experience?
What of this would you like to preserve and develop further?
cards.recommendation:
Choosing what you carry forward restores agency.

Do this (3 days):
1) Write 3 qualities/lessons that strengthened.
2) Pick one.
3) Each day do one small action that expresses it.

This is not about “being stronger”. It’s about being alive and steady.
cards.mechanism: Value consolidation supports identity reconstruction and reduces helplessness.

cards.introduction: Sometimes grief creates a “ban on joy”: if I feel good, I betray the memory. Many men don’t even say it out loud—they just punish themselves by refusing rest, lightness, or pleasure. That doesn’t bring the person back; it only drains you. You can remember and still allow moments of life. In this card you’ll name your fear behind joy and take one small step toward life—carefully, without forcing.
cards.shortDescription: A ban on joy is self-punishment, not loyalty. We’ll take a small, respectful step toward life while keeping memory.
questions: What fears do you have related to returning to joy?
What could be the first small step toward life?
cards.recommendation:
A small step toward life reduces avoidance of positive emotion.

Do this (3 days):
1) Choose one “safe joy” step for 10 minutes.
2) Before: “I remember, and I live.”
3) Do it and rate guilt/shame/warmth 1–10.

Start tiny. Consistency matters more than intensity.
cards.mechanism: Reducing avoidance of positive emotion lowers guilt and supports recovery.
